Q1: I kept things pretty simple and predictable, honestly. I'd set aside time on certain days of the week, usually in the afternoons after my other classes, and just work through whatever needed to be done. I'm a kinesthetic learner so I'd often combine studying with walking around my room or using physical flashcards I made. It wasn't super intense, but it was consistent enough to get me through.
Q2: I tried to stick to regular times because it made everything less stressful for me. Tuesdays and Thursdays were usually my main study days, and Sundays I'd catch up on anything I missed. When deadlines got close I might add an extra session, but I really preferred keeping things predictable. Change makes me anxious, so routine helped me feel more in control.
Q3: Usually it was just habit, like checking it became part of my day the way checking email is. Sometimes I'd log in because I knew an assignment was due soon or I needed to watch a lecture video. Honestly, I didn't always have a specific reason—I'd just open it to make sure I wasn't missing anything important. The fear of falling behind without realizing it kept me checking in.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks it just became automatic. I'd finish lunch, open my laptop, and check the LMS before doing anything else. It happened because I made myself do it at the same time every day until it felt weird not to. Having that routine made me feel less scared about missing something important.
Q5: The lecture videos were probably the most useful because I could pause them and actually practice what was being taught. I learn better when I can do things with my hands or move around, so I'd often follow along with examples while the video played. The discussion posts were okay too because reading other people's perspectives sometimes helped things click for me.
Q6: I tended to skip the optional readings and some of the extra resources they posted. I guess I felt like I had enough to handle with the required stuff, and my motivation wasn't high enough to go beyond what was necessary. If something wasn't going to be graded, I usually told myself I'd get to it later, but I rarely did.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could see how it would help my grade, I was more likely to engage with it. I also valued things that let me physically do something rather than just read passively. Anything that felt too abstract or disconnected from the assessments got pushed aside.
Q8: Optional activities felt like extras I couldn't really afford to spend energy on. With required ones, there's a clear consequence if you don't do them, so I made sure to complete those. Optional stuff felt like a luxury for students who had more motivation or time than me. I always worried I was missing out, but I just couldn't bring myself to do more than necessary.
Q9: Definitely. I downloaded a lot of the PDFs and slides early on and studied from those on my tablet without being connected. I also made physical flashcards and would study those while walking around or doing other things. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer, just working through problems on paper.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend an hour or two going through my printed notes or practicing concepts by hand, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd also discuss things with a friend from class over text or in person, working through problems together. The LMS only captured a fraction of what I was actually doing to learn.
Q11: Yes, all the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, then spend days working through it offline. I preferred having materials saved locally because it felt safer somehow, like I wouldn't lose access. So my one download might represent hours of actual study time that happened later.
Q12: There were moments when I felt really scared and overwhelmed, especially around midterms when everything piled up. During those times, even logging into the LMS felt daunting because I was afraid of seeing more things I hadn't done. The CBT sessions I attended helped a bit, but the fear would still creep in and make it hard to engage.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one for me. With 13 online courses to manage, I often felt stretched thin. When I was tired or had other responsibilities, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS—just enough to not fall behind. My engagement definitely dropped when I was dealing with too many competing demands.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd open a video or reading and just stare at it without really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere, worrying about other assignments or just feeling too tired to focus. I'd technically be logged in and watching, but I wasn't really learning in those moments.
Q15: I avoided discussion posts sometimes even though I knew participation mattered. Posting publicly made me anxious—I was scared of saying something wrong or looking stupid. I'd tell myself I'd do it later when I felt more confident, but then I'd rush it at the last minute or skip it entirely.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main motivator. I'd organize my whole schedule around when things were due and work backward from there. Without a deadline, I probably wouldn't have done much of anything. They created pressure, but that pressure was often the only thing that got me moving.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, but not always positively. When I got good feedback, I felt relieved more than motivated. When feedback was critical, it made me scared to engage with similar tasks again. I'd sometimes delay looking at my grades because I was afraid of what I'd see.
Q18: The structure helped me a lot because I knew what to expect each week. Having a predictable pattern of lectures, readings, and assignments made it easier to build my routine around it. If things had been more chaotic or unpredictable, I think I would have struggled much more.
Q19: The clear weekly modules made engagement easier because I could just follow the path laid out for me. What made it harder was when there were too many optional resources—it made me anxious that I was missing something important even if I couldn't realistically do it all. Simpler would have been better for someone like me.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for something, like they're confused and clicking around trying to understand what they need to do. Or maybe they're anxious like me and keep checking things without actually producing work. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's just nervous energy.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. I got a distinction even though my engagement level was pretty low according to the system. I studied a lot offline and focused on what mattered for exams. The LMS activity doesn't capture the quality of studying, just the quantity of clicks.
Q22: I think logs can show patterns, like when someone is active or how often they visit. But they miss the depth of engagement—whether someone is actually thinking and learning or just going through the motions. They also completely miss offline studying, which for me was where most of my real learning happened.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to check boxes. It's when you're curious and thinking critically, not just scared of failing. For me, true engagement was rare—mostly I was just trying to survive and get through everything without falling apart.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I felt most of the time, or how much effort it took just to maintain my routine. It wouldn't show the physical flashcards I made, the walks I took while reviewing concepts, or the relief I felt when I finished something. My emotional experience of this course was invisible to the data.
